# Transcrição: 2.2-2 Ecommerce Launch Plan

- **Fonte:** 2.2-2 Ecommerce Launch Plan
- **Duração:** 11:31
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Hey, this is Deepash and this is a really short summary video on how I launch e-commerce sites. There's only a few steps to it, but the depth of it is really how this kind of training program has been built out, is to get you to focus on the right steps at the right time and to not try and do everything at the same time. Assuming you've gotten existing e-commerce store and you want to get better traffic, better scale, better optimization, profitability, all your starting from scratch the same applies. The first step is to get the best traffic from Facebook and it's to find the right audience and ad match. When it comes down to Facebook ads, you can only ever get an ad that works for an audience and you'll get an audience that works with an ad. If you get a winning ad, a winning audience, doesn't mean that that ad is always going to work with different audiences. In the same way, if you've got a winning audience, doesn't mean that every single ad you test there is going to work because audiences will differ based on targeting and profile and things like that. The very first thing that I want to do in an e-commerce store is to find the best audience and ad. I do that through graduation testing as part of the whole graduation framework. That's why I put a lot of emphasis here on testing. I will test multiple ads, I will test multiple ad sets and I'm looking for those matches and you can find more details as part of the graduation testing, part of the training. Then once I've got traffic coming through my funnel, which is a decent click through rate, decent cost per click and I'm getting people into my funnel and I can start checking the metrics to see how people are progressing from different parts of the pixels that I'm tracking. So view content, ad to cart, and you check out, etc. All the way through. Then I want to find where the bottlenecks are. There are some instances where I'll start to traffic a site and I'll see that everything is flowing great. Actually the sales funnel is doing great. I've set up the store really well and my numbers are flowing through. But in many cases, especially when I work with clients as opposed to my only comp stores, I find that there are blockers at certain parts. For example, there's a blocker between product page conversion and ad to cart. Once people are into the cart, they're not going ahead and buying. Then it's a case of optimizing your sales funnel. So you've done your traffic testing, you've got some good idea of what's coming through. It doesn't mean that it's the best traffic. It could also be a traffic problem. But the good thing is that when you've got multiple audiences running, you can actually look at these metrics at the kind of ad set level. And you'll see if this is generically across all ad sets, in which case it's a sales funnel problem, or if it's specific ad sets, in which cases it's a traffic problem. So there's just something to bear in mind. So once you find the problem area, then you can do something about it. So in most cases, it'll be your product page that's playing up. And there is a training video on some recommendations for product page optimization and some ideas there as well.

## [03:03 - 06:03]

But really, for your particular store, it's going to be about testing and analysis. So the kind of an analysis I like to do is to run surveys or polls and find out why people are not buying. The most powerful question I've found is, is there anything stopping you from buying today? I usually run that through hotjar. Some people prefer lucky or in you might have your own preference for how you run that poll. But it's literally a question that pops up on the product page. And often on the cart page, just asking people what's blocking them. And you'll get a ton of information from that as well. You can also record user sessions. So again, I use hotjar. You can use Lucky Orange or another tool to see how people are clicking and navigating around that page to see if there's any obvious blockers. Often people will use the mouse to browse over the things that they're looking at at the moment in time or thinking about. So you can see, for example, if someone's hovering over a particular area, they seem to take ages to progress. Maybe that's a problem. Then you take your insights and you create a B split tests. So you can use a tool like visual website optimizer, optimizely, Google website optimizer. There's lots of different A B test tools out there. Maybe even your Econ platform has it as well. And what I would do is to start asking questions of your product page. So what if I move this? What if I actually change the color of this and use your A B testing to answer those questions? So just like in a science test, when you're kind of in school, you have your hypotheses and then you have your conclusions. And what you're trying to do is to funnel more and more traffic through your sales funnel. Or conversely, you want to plug all those leaky gaps in your funnel. So part two is really about maximizing your conversion rate for the funnel. Once you've got conversion rates starting to improve, then your cost per acquisition and your return on ad spend numbers will naturally start to improve as well. So recently I've been working on a particular store of mine and I knew that the product page was the blocker based on the kind of analysis I teach. And through running curve A B tests and hot draw analysis, I've almost doubled conversion rate and it's halved my cost per acquisition and it's almost doubled my return on ad spend. And that's the key thing here. So Facebook isn't always your problem. Quite often it's your sales funnel and I don't see enough people focusing enough on here. So this is a Facebook ads training calls, but it's about squeezing as much value out of your e-commerce setup as possible. So that's two parts and then kind of see PNR start to improve. That's when you start to tweak and optimize what you're getting. Then you what I would do at that point is to then think about whether I can actually do anything to influence my AOV. In many cases, you know, you start selling a single product, let's say it's $25, maybe even $75. And depending on how competitive your niche is, your gross, your return on aspen might not be as profitable as you need it to be.

## [06:03 - 09:05]

If it is, then great, you can skip AOV optimization, but sometimes it's not. And you need to then think about, right, how can I bump up my average order value? It could be pre-purchase upsells or cross sells. So an upsell is more of the same generally or a more expensive version of the same product. For example, if you're selling watches, it could be two watches or it could be the next model up. Cross sell is selling related products. So if you're selling watches, a cross sell could be a strap. So there are different ways of bumping up your average order value on your purchase. Then once you're comfortable with the CPI and ROS numbers, then that's the point I look at scaling. And this is often where I see people failing is they haven't really worked on the sales funnel metrics and optimized conversion. They're not really happy with the AOV. The ROS isn't so great. They're really keen to start scaling up and start spending more money because obviously that's how things work with Facebook, but reality is it's not. And if you don't really work those previous steps successfully, you will struggle. And the final piece of this puzzle, and you could argue it happens before scaling for some people, for others it is after, is lifetime value. Lifetime value is thinking about getting people to buy more frequently from you. So after they make their first purchase, get them to buy their second one quickly and the third one quickly. Or it could be about selling more products to them, maybe less frequently. So it could be that they're bought a watch now and in two, three months time you release a new version or not a new version. Or maybe it's a new line, for example, and you know that they, having bought the product, they might be interested in it when you remark it to them again. And actually the difference really between AOV and LTV is AOV is generally influenced with things on your website. It can be influenced also by abandoned cart and messenger and also potentially your dynamic product ads. But LTV is after they've made their first purchase. So lifetime value is about they are now a customer. How can you squeeze more value out of them? And it's either to buy more frequently or to spend more money with you and obviously a combination of both. So if someone's bought a watch and you know that on average, you're selling a $200 watch and your lifetime value, which you've calculated over, I don't know, 18 months of operation is $350, then you have an idea of what value of a single customer is. So when you're talking about cost fragmentation and return on ad spend, if it costs you $100 to acquire that customer, they buy a $200 product. And let's say your profit on that is maybe $50, then you know that over the next 12, 18 months, because you've got programs in place, like for example, email sequences, you might be doing Facebook lies, maybe have other promotions going for existing customers,

## [09:05 - 11:31]

you're going to bump up that value to $350, then you know that that initial $100 investment is actually going to bring back an even better return. So that's really the formula that I work on for an e-commerce launch is really kind of working on each of those steps. And only until I've worked all the way through one and seven, am I really confident that I can scale up, you know, not even talk about some figures, I'm talking about eight figure e-commerce business this year. So, you know, if you're starting out, then steps one, two, three, and four are really the things that you really have to get right to take you to five figures, six figures, and kind of be able to build that up in terms of profitability and things like that. But if you're really looking for seven figures, you want to start scaling, if you haven't done steps one to five efficiently and properly, you're going to struggle. And then moving a company from seven figures to multiple seven figures, and then into eight figures, you want to work on your lifetime value like an ideal balance between new customer acquisition and existing customer revenue, because there's something around 70-30 ratio. So you want to get to a level where 70% of your revenue is coming through new acquisition because you want to keep that pipeline going. Then you want 30% of your revenue coming from existing customers. That gives you a super healthy profitability balance where you're able to keep on bringing in new customers, and you're able to keep monetizing your existing customers. And that will differ across niche and price points and things like that. So it's just a rough number for you to consider. But really, that's the kind of core guts of getting your e-com site working and getting it working with Facebook ads. Facebook ads is only a mechanism to drive traffic. This e-commerce launch plan is something I've been focusing on for 15 years, regardless of traffic. 15 years ago, Facebook didn't exist. I was doing affiliate marketing, search engine optimization, paid search, TV ads, direct mail, lots of other things. Facebook has now come along, and it's the fastest channel online, I think, to scale with. It's also still so much opportunity for e-commerce as well. So Facebook is really just a part of step one. After that, it's all about being clever with your store and your e-commerce marketing to get the maximum value back from your investment in your advertising.
